content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Theresa Fuentes 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Pasadena 
100 N. Garfield Avenue, Suite N210 
Pasadena, CA 91109 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-171 
Dear Ms. Fuentes: 
October 1, 2018 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of Pasadena City owicilmember Andy 
Wilson and Mayor Terry Tomek for advic regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accw·ate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Councilmember Wilson and Mayor Tomek participate in the signature gathering 
process required to form a landmark district, given the location of their respective residences? 
2. Does Councilmember Wilson have a conflict of interest in considering the potential 
designation of a landmark district in the City of Pasadena, where he owns a home within the 
boundaries of tbe district and his home is considered a "contributing" structure to the district? 
3. Does Mayor Tomek have a conflict of interest in considering the potential designation of 
a landmark district in the City of Pasadena, where he owns a home within the boundaries of a 
smaller landmark district that will be surrounded by the new district? 
1 The Poli1ical Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in ections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-18-171 
Page No. 2 
1. Nothing in the Act would prohibit Councilmernber Wilson and Mayor Tomek from 
participating in the signature gathering process, as this does not involve a governmental decision. 
2 & 3. Councilrnember Wilson and Mayor Tomek each have a "financial interest" in real 
property that would result in a conflict of interest. They may not take part in the decision 
concerning the designation of the landmark district. 
FACTS 
The City of Pasadena is considering designating a particular residential neighborhood as a 
new landmark district under its zoning code, the Madi on Heights Landmark District (' MHLD"). 
Four smaUer landmark districts already exist within the proposed boundaries of the MHLD. You 
note that at this time the four smaller districts will remain and will not be subsi.µned by the MHLD. 
Each of the existing small districts run the length of a city block and include the properties on 
either ide of the street. 
Councilmember Wilson's residence is not located in one of the existing districts, but would 
be included within the boundaries of the proposed MHLD. His home is also considered a 
"contributing" structure to the district, which means that preserving its current exterior appearance 
and quality is important to the integrity of the district and would require a discretionary 
permit ifhe were to alter the exterior. You note that being located within a landmark district is 
touted by realtors as a valuable attribute when selling a home on the theory that the district 
designation ensures stability in neighborhood character. 
Mayor Tomek's residence is located within one of the small existing historic districts. It is 
within 500 feet of properties that are not currently a part of any landmark district, but would 
become a part of the MHLD. While you state that it "appears likely that any real estate value in 
being in a district is already experienced by Mayor Tomek," you also suggest ' that increasing by a 
substantial amount the area of homes around his that are also within a district may be beneficial to 
his home value." 
Consideration of designation of the MHLD begins with a petition by the neighborhood to 
the City for designation. A majority of the property owners within the district must sign the petition 
in order for it to be valid. Councilmember Wilson and Mayor Tomek would like to participate in 
the signature gathering process in their private capacity as residents of the impacted neighborhood. 
The petition and other application materials must first be reviewed by the City's Historic 
Preservation Commission, which will then make a recommendation to the City Council on whether 
to adopt the MHLD. The City Council, of which Councilmember Wilson and Mayor Tomek are 
voting members, will vote whether to adopt the MHLD. 
ANALYSIS 
The primary pw-pose of the Act s conflict of interest provisions is to ensure that 'public 
officials whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias 
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported 


File No. A-18-171 
Page No. 3 
them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public 
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 
provides that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family or on one or more of the 
official's financial interests, including an interest in real property. (Section 87103.) 
Petition Signature Gathering 
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. 
As mentioned above, the Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only when a public 
official "make[s], participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his [or her] official 
position to influence a governmental decision in which he [ or she] knows or has reason to know he 
[or she] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2). The Commission has 
defined "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision. 
(Regulation 18704.) 
Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision" when 
the official, "official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits 
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his 
or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(a).) 
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in a 
governmental decision if the official-provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the 
purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Section 
87100; Regulation 18704(b).) 
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules that address whether a public 
official is using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public 
official's own agency or "an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her 
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision." In these cases, if the official "contacts or appears 
before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the 
agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a governmental decision. (Regulation 
18704(c)(l).) 
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency other 
than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control 
of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or 
purport "to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact" 
to influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his or her interests. 


File No. A-18-171 
Page No. 4 
In this case the gathering of signatures on a petition to be submitted to the City is not a 
"governmental decision" for the purposes of the Act and the Act does not prohibit Councilmember 
Wilson and Mayor Tomek from participation in signature gathering while acting in their private 
capacities. 
Landmark District Designation 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
We must determine whether the financial effect of the decisions on Councilmember Wilson 
and Mayor Tornek's real property interests is both foreseeable and material. 
Councilmember Wilson: 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision as a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870 I (a).) An 
interest is the "subject" of a proceeding if the decision involves any license permit or other 
entitlement to or contract with the interest including a decision affecting real property under 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). Regulation l 8702.2(a)(2) provides that a real property ~terest, other 
than a leasehold interest, is the subject of the decision whenever the governmental decision 
"determjnes the parcel's in9lusionor exclusion from any city, county, district, or other local 
government subdivision, or other boundaries ... " Because decisions regarding the creation of the 
MHLD involve the determination of the boundaries of a new district which would include 
Councilmember Wilson's residence, his interest is explicitly involved in the decisions and the 
financial effect of the decisjons on his property is both foreseeable and material. Accordingly, 
ouncilrnember Wilson may not make participate, or use his official positon to influence the 
decision. 
Mayor Tornek: 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, such as Mayor 
Tornek's residence, Regulation 18701(b) provides that the decision's financial effi ct is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Materiality 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. As relevant to the facts here, the 
financial effect of the decisions on the officials' respective real property interests is material if the 
decisions: 
• 
Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the 
property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property containing a 
business entity where the materiality tandards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. 
Notwithstanding this prorubition the Commission may provide written advice allowing an 
official to participate under these circumstanc s if the Commission determine that there are 


' 
FileNo.A-18-171 
Page No. 5 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on 
the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l l).) 
• 
Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a: nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
(Regulati~m 18702.2(a)(12).) 
Mayor Tomek resides on a street that is located within an existing landmark district. His 
residence is located within 500 feet of various properties that are not currently lo~ated in a 
landmark district, but would become a part of the proposed MHLD. You have indicated "that 
increasing by a substantial amount the area of homes around his that are also within a district may 
be beneficial to his home value." The existing district in which Mayor Tornek's residence is located 
is, in fact, very small, consisting of homes located on either side of a single block of a single street. 
The proposed MHLD would be considerably larger, and include many square blocks of homes 
around his in one contiguous district. As such, the facts in this case do not establish that the 
decisions in question will have no measurable impact. Consequently, Mayor Tomek has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest in the decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~~~~ 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
